questions.prompt: How do you say 'Good day' in Croatian?
questions.explanation: 'Dobar dan' = good day (the standard daytime greeting). 'Laku noć' = good night, 'Doviđenja' = goodbye, 'Hvala' = thank you.

questions.prompt: Which word means 'thank you'?
questions.explanation: 'Hvala' = thank you. 'Molim' = please/you're welcome, 'Oprostite' = excuse me, 'Bok' = hi/bye.

questions.prompt: The letter 'š' is pronounced like...
questions.explanation: Croatian is phonetic: š = 'sh', č/ć = 'ch', ž = 'zh', j = 'y'. 'škola' = SHKOH-lah (school).

questions.prompt: Complete the introduction: 'Ja ___ Marko.' (I am Marko)
questions.explanation: biti short forms: ja sam, ti si, on je... With 'ja' it's 'sam'. 'Ja sam Marko' = I am Marko.

questions.prompt: What gender is 'žena' (woman)?
questions.explanation: Nouns ending in -a are usually feminine. Consonant endings → masculine; -o/-e → neuter.

questions.prompt: Write the number 'three' in Croatian.
questions.explanation: 0-5: nula, jedan, dva, tri, četiri, pet. 'Tri' = three.

questions.prompt: Choose the negative of 'je' (is): 'Ona ___ ovdje.' (She is NOT here)
questions.explanation: biti has one-word negatives: nisam, nisi, nije, nismo, niste, nisu. For 'ona' (she) it's 'nije'.

questions.prompt: Conjugate raditi (to work) for 'ja': 'Ja ___.'
questions.explanation: Present endings: -m, -š, –, -mo, -te, -e/ju. raditi → radim, radiš, radi, radimo, radite, rade.

questions.prompt: Croatian has no articles. So 'kuća' can mean...
questions.explanation: There is no 'a' or 'the' in Croatian. Context tells you which English article fits. 'kuća' = (a/the) house.

questions.prompt: Order the words: 'I speak a little Croatian.'
questions.explanation: The verb 'govorim' already means 'I speak' (no pronoun needed). 'malo' = a little, 'hrvatski' = Croatian.

questions.prompt: Conjugate imati (to have) for 'ja': 'Ja ___ brata.' (I have a brother)
questions.explanation: imati: imam, imaš, ima, imamo, imate, imaju. With 'ja' → 'imam'.

questions.prompt: Put 'kava' (coffee, feminine) into the accusative: 'Pijem ___.' (I drink coffee)
questions.explanation: Feminine -a nouns change to -u in the accusative (the object). 'Pijem kavu' = I drink coffee.

questions.prompt: Make a yes/no question: 'Govoriš engleski' → ?
questions.explanation: Add 'li' right after the verb to form a yes/no question: 'Govoriš li engleski?' = Do you speak English?

questions.prompt: Negate the verb: 'Ja ___ razumijem.' (I do NOT understand)
questions.explanation: Regular verbs negate with 'ne' before the verb: 'Ne razumijem' = I don't understand. (biti is the exception with nisam etc.)

questions.prompt: Choose the future tense: 'Sutra ___ raditi.' (Tomorrow I will work)
questions.explanation: Future = short htjeti (ću, ćeš, će...) + infinitive. 'Ja ću raditi' / 'Radit ću' = I will work.

questions.prompt: Past tense, male speaker: 'Jučer ___ radio.' (Yesterday I worked)
questions.explanation: Perfekt = short biti (sam, si, je...) + participle. 'Radio sam' = I worked (man). A woman says 'Radila sam'.

questions.prompt: Which past participle is correct for a FEMALE speaker saying 'I worked'?
questions.explanation: The participle agrees with gender: -o (m), -la (f), -lo (n), -li/-le (pl). A woman says 'radila sam'.

questions.prompt: Genitive: 'a glass of water' — complete 'čaša ___.' (voda → genitive)
questions.explanation: Feminine -a → -e in the genitive. 'čaša vode' = a glass of water. Genitive expresses 'of'.

questions.prompt: Aspect: which verb means a COMPLETED action ('to drink up')?
questions.explanation: piti (imperfective) = to be drinking / drink habitually; popiti (perfective, with prefix po-) = to drink up completely.

questions.prompt: Order the words: 'The woman drinks coffee.' (use the correct cases)
questions.explanation: 'Žena' stays nominative (the subject), 'kavu' is accusative (the object), 'pije' = drinks. Word order is flexible, but this is the neutral order.

questions.prompt: Genitive of possession: 'This is the friend ___.' (of my brother — brat → genitive)
questions.explanation: Masculine nouns add -a in the genitive: brat → brata. The genitive expresses 'of' / possession: 'prijatelj moga brata'.

questions.prompt: Locative (location): 'I live in the city.' Complete: 'Živim u ___.' (grad → locative)
questions.explanation: After 'u/na' meaning a static location, use the locative: grad → gradu. 'Živim u gradu' = I live in the city.

questions.prompt: Which verb is PERFECTIVE (a single completed action)?
questions.explanation: napisati (with prefix na-) is perfective = to write and finish. pisati is imperfective = to be writing / write habitually.

questions.prompt: Past tense, female speaker: 'Jučer sam ___ u školi.' (was — biti participle, feminine)
questions.explanation: The past participle agrees with gender: bio (m), bila (f), bilo (n). A woman says 'bila sam' = I was.

questions.prompt: Dative (to whom): 'I'm giving the book to my friend.' 'Dajem knjigu ___.' (prijatelj → dative)
questions.explanation: The dative marks the recipient ('to/for'): prijatelj → prijatelju. 'Dajem knjigu prijatelju' = I give the book to my friend.

questions.prompt: Match each case to what it marks.
questions.explanation: Each case marks a role: nominative = subject, accusative = direct object, genitive = of/possession, dative = recipient. Endings change to show the role.

questions.prompt: Future tense: 'Tomorrow I will study.' Choose: 'Sutra ___ učiti.'
questions.explanation: Future = short htjeti (ću, ćeš, će...) + infinitive: 'Ja ću učiti' / 'Učit ću'.

questions.prompt: Instrumental ('with'): 'I travel with a friend.' 'Putujem s ___.' (prijatelj → instrumental)
questions.explanation: The instrumental marks 'with' / means: prijatelj → prijateljem (masculine -em after a soft consonant). 's prijateljem' = with a friend.

questions.prompt: Comparative: 'Croatia is ___ than Slovenia.' (bigger — velik)
questions.explanation: Comparative of velik is irregular: veći (bigger). najveći is the superlative (biggest). Use 'nego/od' for 'than'.

questions.prompt: Order the words: 'Yesterday I drank coffee in a café.' (past + accusative + locative)
questions.answer: Jučer sam pio kavu u kafiću
questions.explanation: Past = 'sam pio' (male). 'kavu' is accusative (object), 'kafiću' is locative (location after 'u'). Three systems working together.

questions.prompt: Conditional ('would'): 'I would like a coffee.' Choose: 'Ja ___ kavu.'
questions.explanation: The conditional = aorist of biti (bih, bi, bi, bismo, biste, bi) + participle. 'Htio bih kavu' = I would like a coffee (polite).

questions.prompt: Vocative (addressing): call out to Marko. 'Hej, ___!'
questions.explanation: Many masculine names like Marko keep the same form in the vocative, but most masculine nouns change: prijatelj → prijatelju! The vocative is only for addressing someone.

questions.prompt: Which case after the preposition 'iz' (out of)? 'Dolazim iz ___.' (grad)
questions.explanation: 'iz' (from/out of) always takes the genitive: iz grada = out of the city. Many prepositions govern a fixed case.

questions.prompt: Plural accusative: 'I see the children.' 'Vidim ___.' (djeca → accusative)
questions.explanation: 'djeca' (children) is a collective noun behaving like a feminine singular: accusative djecu. 'Vidim djecu' = I see the children.

questions.prompt: Aspect choice: 'I read (and finished) the book yesterday.' Pick the verb.
questions.explanation: A completed, whole action takes the perfective: pročitati → pročitao sam. 'čitao sam' (imperfective) means 'I was reading / used to read'.

questions.prompt: Match each preposition to the case it governs.
questions.explanation: Prepositions govern specific cases — and some (like 'u') take different cases for motion (accusative) vs location (locative). This is central to B1 accuracy.

questions.prompt: Dative plural: 'I'm helping my friends.' 'Pomažem ___.' (prijatelji → dative)
questions.explanation: The dative plural ending is -ima for masculine/feminine: prijatelji → prijateljima. The verb 'pomagati' takes the dative.

questions.prompt: Conditional 'if' sentence: 'If I had money, I would travel.' The 'would travel' part is...
questions.explanation: Hypothetical result uses the conditional: 'Da imam novca, putovao bih.' (bih + participle).

questions.prompt: Comparative + 'than': 'Zagreb is bigger than Split.' 'Zagreb je veći ___ Split.'
questions.explanation: After a comparative, 'than' = 'od' (+ genitive) or 'nego'. 'veći od Splita' / 'veći nego Split'.

questions.prompt: Order the words: 'I told my friend that I would come.' (dative + conditional)
questions.answer: Rekao sam prijatelju da ću doći
questions.explanation: 'prijatelju' is dative (told TO my friend); 'da ću doći' = that I will come. Croatian often uses the future where English uses 'would' in reported speech.

questions.prompt: Clitic placement (2nd position): which is correct for 'I gave it to him'?
questions.explanation: Unstressed clitics cluster in second position in a fixed order: verb-aux 'sam', then dative 'mu', then accusative 'ga'. Clitic ordering is a hallmark B2 skill.

questions.prompt: Aspect: 'I read for an hour (process, didn't necessarily finish).' 'Sat vremena sam ___.' (čitati, male)
questions.explanation: A process over a span uses the imperfective: 'čitao sam' (was reading). The perfective 'pročitao' would imply completion of the whole book.

questions.prompt: Passive (participle): 'The letter ___ yesterday.' (was written — pismo, neuter)
questions.explanation: The passive uses biti + passive participle agreeing with the subject: pismo (neuter) → 'napisano'. 'Pismo je napisano' = the letter was written.

questions.prompt: Genitive plural: 'a lot of books' — 'puno ___.' (knjiga → genitive plural)
questions.explanation: Many feminine -a nouns have a zero-ending genitive plural identical to nominative singular: knjiga. Quantities take the genitive: 'puno knjiga'.

questions.prompt: Reflexive 'se' for impersonal: 'How ___ this said in Croatian?'
questions.explanation: The reflexive 'se' forms impersonal/passive-like statements: 'Kako se kaže...?' = How does one say / how is it said...?

questions.prompt: Match each imperfective verb to its perfective partner.
questions.explanation: Aspect pairs are often unpredictable: some add a prefix (pisati/napisati), some change the root (govoriti/reći, dolaziti/doći). Learning pairs together is essential at B2.

questions.prompt: Conditional perfect: 'I would have come.' Choose:
questions.explanation: The past conditional = bio/bila + bih + participle: 'bio bih došao' = I would have come. 'došao bih' is the present conditional ('I would come').

questions.prompt: Instrumental of means without preposition: 'I write with a pen.' 'Pišem ___.' (olovka → instrumental)
questions.explanation: The bare instrumental (no preposition) expresses the means/tool: olovka → olovkom. 'Pišem olovkom' = I write with a pen. ('s' is only for accompaniment.)

questions.prompt: Which preposition+case for 'I'm going to the doctor's' (a person, motion to)?
questions.explanation: Motion to a person uses 'kod' + genitive (idem kod liječnika) or 'k' + dative. You don't use 'u/na' with people the way you do with places.

questions.prompt: Order the clitic cluster: 'I have already given it to her.'
questions.explanation: After the first word 'Već', the clitic chain follows in fixed order: aux 'sam' → dative 'joj' → accusative 'ga', then the participle 'dao'.

questions.prompt: Recognise the tense: 'Bijah umoran.' What is 'bijah'?
questions.explanation: The imperfekt (bijah = I was) is an archaic/literary past, now rare in speech but met in literature and elevated style. C1 means recognising it.

questions.prompt: The aorist survives in speech mainly with which verb? 'Rekoh ti!'
questions.explanation: Though largely literary, the aorist lingers colloquially in fixed forms like 'rekoh' (I said) and 'dođoh'. Spotting it is a C1 reading skill.

questions.prompt: Idiom: complete 'Pada kiša kao iz ___.' (raining as if from buckets/'a jug')
questions.explanation: 'Kao iz kabla' = (pouring) as if from a bucket — the Croatian equivalent of 'raining cats and dogs'. Idiom is central to C1.

questions.prompt: Verbal adverb (gerund): 'Reading the book, he fell asleep.' Choose 'reading'.
questions.explanation: The present verbal adverb (glagolski prilog sadašnji) ends in -ći: 'čitajući' = while reading. It compresses a simultaneous clause, marking polished style.

questions.prompt: Word formation: form the abstract noun from 'sloboda'-style — give the noun from the adjective 'slobodan' (free).
questions.explanation: slobodan (free, adj.) → sloboda (freedom, noun). C1 involves productive word-formation: recognising and building related words across parts of speech.

questions.prompt: Match each idiom to its meaning.

pairs.left: ni luk jeo ni luk mirisao

questions.explanation: Native idioms with no literal English equivalent are a clear C1 marker; using them naturally signals near-native command.

questions.prompt: Choose the stylistically richer synonym for 'velik' (big) meaning 'enormous':
questions.explanation: C1 means a graded vocabulary: 'golem' (enormous) is a stronger, more vivid choice than the neutral 'velik'. Lexical precision matters.

questions.prompt: Ekavian vs Ijekavian: the Croatian (ijekavian) word for 'milk' is 'mlijeko'; give the word for 'child'.
questions.explanation: Standard Croatian is ijekavian (mlijeko, dijete) vs Serbian ekavian (mleko, dete). Controlling this reflex distinguishes polished Croatian at C1.

questions.prompt: Which shows correct fronting for emphasis ('It's HIM I saw')?
questions.explanation: Croatian's flexible word order lets you front the stressed full pronoun 'njega' for emphasis, with the clitic 'sam' still in second position.

questions.prompt: Order this elevated sentence: 'Although tired, he kept working.' (verbal adverb + clitic)
questions.explanation: An elliptical concessive ('Iako umoran' = although [he was] tired) plus the clitic 'je' in second position of the main clause — concise, native-like phrasing.
